title: Blind Spot Check Guide
summary: Understand how area size, camera field of view, target distance, camera count, and overlap affect uncovered coverage zones.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether a planned camera group leaves meaningful uncovered area after view width and overlap assumptions are applied.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when checking a camera layout for missed zones, especially after initial field-of-view, spacing, or coverage-area planning.
bullets: Early design planning.
Camera placement review.
Expansion or retrofit validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical physical security planning terms.

items.label: Area Width (ft)
items.description: The width of the area being checked for coverage gaps.
items.whyItMatters: Area width defines the horizontal coverage requirement the camera group must satisfy.
items.commonMistake: Checking only the obvious center area and missing side edges or transition zones.

items.label: Area Depth (ft)
items.description: The depth of the area being checked for coverage gaps.
items.whyItMatters: Depth completes the area being evaluated for blind spots.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring depth where objects, people, or vehicles can move outside the main view.

items.label: Camera Horizontal FOV (deg)
items.description: The side-to-side view angle used by the cameras in the blind spot check.
items.whyItMatters: Horizontal FOV controls the estimated coverage width of each camera at the target distance.
items.commonMistake: Using a wide angle to erase blind spots without checking detail loss.

links.label: Verify the view geometry first
links.note: Field of View confirms the coverage width behind the blind spot estimate.

items.label: Mounting Distance to Target Zone (ft)
items.description: The distance from the camera mounting position to the main target zone being covered.
items.whyItMatters: Distance determines how wide each camera view becomes at the target zone.
items.commonMistake: Using the camera-to-ground distance instead of the camera-to-target-zone distance.

items.label: Number of Cameras
items.description: The number of camera views included in the coverage estimate.
items.whyItMatters: Camera count affects total coverage and overlap opportunities.
items.commonMistake: Counting cameras that do not actually point at the same target zone or have blocked views.

items.label: Overlap Target (%)
items.description: The desired overlap between camera views.
items.whyItMatters: Overlap reduces seam risk and helps absorb aiming variation or field conditions.
items.commonMistake: Treating edge-to-edge calculated coverage as if it has no gaps.

links.label: Review repeated spacing strategy
links.note: Camera Spacing helps turn overlap goals into placement spacing.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the entered layout appears to have manageable uncovered area. Watch means overlap, mounting, or obstruction assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the design may leave unacceptable blind spots or require more cameras, repositioning, or narrower spacing.
bullets: Blind spot checks are strongest when based on a site plan or measured target zones.
Obstructions, corners, glare, mounting height, and door swings can create blind spots that simple geometry misses.
Use this as a layout review step after estimating FOV, spacing, and coverage area.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most physical security planning problems come from treating camera coverage, recognition detail, mounting position, and lighting as separate decisions. In the field, those choices interact: a wider view lowers pixel density, a higher mount changes target geometry, and poor lighting can erase detail that looked acceptable on paper.
bullets: Sizing a camera view only by how wide the scene looks, without checking target detail.
Using clean daytime assumptions for a scene that must also work at night, in glare, or under motion.
Ignoring overlap, obstructions, mounting limits, and the actual target plane.
Treating calculated values as final design approval instead of documented planning assumptions.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: These guides explain the planning logic behind the tool. They do not replace manufacturer documentation, site survey, camera testing, client acceptance criteria, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera model, lens, sensor, encoder settings, and mounting location.
Confirm lighting, reflections, obstructions, weather, and target movement before relying on a design.
Use calculated outputs as design evidence and review notes, not as a substitute for field validation.
Leave practical margin when the scene is important, difficult, or expected to change.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, on-site testing, legal review, privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Document assumptions before implementation.
Review the design with the owner, integrator, manufacturer, and applicable authority when required.
Test representative views before treating a camera placement as final.
